aStats 2 : Probability
1. Experiment & Outcomes 2. Mathematical Theory 3. Sample Space 4. Events 5. Probability Function 6. Probability Space 7. Properties 8. Conditional Probability 9. Mutually Disjoint event 10. Total Probability 11. Bayes' Theorem 12. Independence of Events 13. Random Variable 14. Countable & Uncountable Sets (Informal) 15. Discrete Random Variable 16. Continuous Random Variable 17. Joint Distribution 18. Marginal Distribution 19. Conditional Distribution 20. Independent Random Variables 21. Independent & Identically Distributed (iid) Random Variables 22. Expectation & Variance 23. Covariance & Correlartion
1. Experiment & OutcomesExperiment : An experiment is a process or phenomenon that we wish to study statistically.• Tossing a coin• Rolling a die Outcome : Result of an experiment• Tossing a coin - Head, Tail• Rolling a die - 1,2,3,4,5 & 61,2,3,4,5&6
2. Mathematical TheoryIn a mathematical theory, we• precisely define the objects of interest• make a few assumptions, which are called as axioms• develop all further results through logical deduction. Probability Theory : It is a mathematical theory for describing the likelihood of events in an experiment. Here, the objects of intersts are::• Sample Space : S:S• Event Space : F:F • Probability Function P:F→[0,1]P:F→[0,1] These are defined in the upcoming sections.
3. Sample SpaceA sample space is a set that contains all the outcomes.• Tossing a coin - {
Heads
,
Tails
}{Heads,Tails}• Rolling a die - {1,2,3,4,5,6}{1,2,3,4,5,6}
4. EventsAn event is a collection of outcomes. An event is a subset of sample space. • Rolling a die - Outcome is less than 44– {1,2,3}{1,2,3}• Set operations such as union, intersection, and complement can be applied to events Event Space : The set of all the events, denoted by FF, is the power set of the sample space.
5. Probability FunctionP:F→[0,1]P:F→[0,1] is a probability function if it satisfies the following axioms.• Axiom-1 : For any event AA, P(A)⩾0P(A)⩾0• Axiom-2 : P(S)=1P(S)=1• Axiom-3 : If E1,...,EnE1,...,En are disjoint event, then P(E1∪...∪En)=P(E1)+....+P(En)P(E1∪...∪En)=P(E1)+....+P(En)
6. Probability SpaceThe following tuple is reffered to as a probability space.⟨S,F,P⟩⟨S,F,P⟩where,• SS is the sample space of an experiment• FF is the power set of the sample• PP is the probability function
7. PropertiesProperty 1 : Let AA be an event. Then, P(AC)=1-P(A)P(AC)=1-P(A)Proof : AA and ACAC are disjoint. Also, S=A∪ACS=A∪AC.
P(S)=1
P(A∪Ac)=1
P(A)+P(AC)=1
P(AC)=1-P(A)
P(S)=1P(A∪Ac)=1P(A)+P(AC)=1P(AC)=1-P(A) Property 2 : P(ϕ)=0P(ϕ)=0, where ϕϕ is the null set.Proof :
P(SC)=1-P(S)
P(ϕ)=1-P(S)
P(ϕ)=0
P(SC)=1-P(S)P(ϕ)=1-P(S)P(ϕ)=0 Property 3 : If event BB is a subset of event AA, then, P(B)⩽P(A)P(B)⩽P(A)Proof : Since B⊆AB⊆A,A=B∪BCA=B∪BCAlso, P(BC)⩾0P(BC)⩾0
P(B∪BC)=P(A)
P(B)+P(BC)=P(A)
P(B)⩽P(A)
P(B∪BC)=P(A)P(B)+P(BC)=P(A)P(B)⩽P(A) Property 4 : If AA and BB are two events, then• P(A)=P(A∩B)+P(A\B)P(A)=P(A∩B)+P(A\B)• P(B)=P(A∩B)+P(B\A)P(B)=P(A∩B)+P(B\A)Proof : Set AA and set BB can be express as a union of two disjoint sets.A=(A∩B)∪(A\B)B=(A∩B)∪(B\A)A=(A∩B)∪(A\B)B=(A∩B)∪(B\A)By axiom-3, the given property is true. Property 5 : If AA and BB are two events, then P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B)P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B)Proof : A∪BA∪B can be expressed as a union of three disjoint set.A∪B=(A\B)∪(B\A)∪(A∩B)A∪B=(A\B)∪(B\A)∪(A∩B)By property-4,• P(A)-P(A∩B)=P(A\B)P(A)-P(A∩B)=P(A\B)• P(B)-P(A∩B)=P(B\A)P(B)-P(A∩B)=P(B\A)
P(A∪B)=P(A\B)+P(B\A)+P(A∩B)
=P(A)-P(A∩B)+P(B)-P(A∩B)+P(A∩B)
=P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B)
P(A∪B)=P(A\B)+P(B\A)+P(A∩B)=P(A)-P(A∩B)+P(B)-P(A∩B)+P(A∩B)=P(A)+P(B)-P(A∩B) Similarly, one can show thatP(A∪B∪C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A∩B)-P(B∩C)-P(A∩C)+P(A∩B∩C)P(A∪B∪C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A∩B)-P(B∩C)-P(A∩C)+P(A∩B∩C)
8. Conditional ProbabilityGiven that event BB has occurred, the probability of event AA occurring is given byP(A|B)=P(A∩B)
P(B)P(A|B)=P(A∩B)P(B)
SSBBAAA∩BA∩B
9. Mutually Disjoint eventB1,...,BnB1,...,Bn are mutually disjoint events ifBi∩Bj=ϕ ∀i,j∈{1,..,n}&i≠jBi∩Bj=ϕ∀i,j∈{1,..,n}&i≠j
10. Total ProbabilitySuppose.A=(A∩B1)∪(A∩B2)∪...∪(A∩Bn)A=(A∩B1)∪(A∩B2)∪...∪(A∩Bn) where, B1,...,BnB1,...,Bn are mutually disjoint events and if B1∪...∪Bn=SB1∪...∪Bn=S then, P(A)=P(A∩B1)+P(A∩B2)+...+P(A∩Bn)P(A)=P(A∩B1)+P(A∩B2)+...+P(A∩Bn)
SSAAB1B1B2B2B3B3B4B4B5B5B6B6B7B7B8B8
11. Bayes' TheoremLet AA and BB be events with P(A)>0P(A)>0 and P(B)>0P(B)>0. Then,P(A|B)×P(B)=P(B|A)×P(A)P(A|B)×P(B)=P(B|A)×P(A) This is coming from conditional probabiltiyP(A|B)×P(B)=P(A∩B)=P(B|A)×P(A)P(A|B)×P(B)=P(A∩B)=P(B|A)×P(A)
12. Independence of EventsTwo events AA and BB are independent ifP(A∩B)=P(A)×P(B)P(A∩B)=P(A)×P(B) Independence of two events implies that the occurrence of one event does not change the chance of occurrence of the other event.P(A|B)=P(A∩B)
P(B)=P(A)×P(B)
P(B)=P(A)P(A|B)=P(A∩B)P(B)=P(A)×P(B)P(B)=P(A)Similarly, P(B|A)=P(B)P(B|A)=P(B) Note : Independent events need not be disjoint events. If two event MM and NN are disjoint, thenP(M∩N)=P(ϕ)=0P(M∩N)=P(ϕ)=0
13. Random VariableA random variable is a function with domain as the sample space and codomain as the real numbers. Example• Experiment : Tossing two coins.• Sample Space : S={HH,HT,TH,TT}S={HH,HT,TH,TT}• Random Variable : X:S→RX:S→R, denotes the number of heads• X(HH)=2X(HH)=2• X(HT)=1X(HT)=1• X(TH)=1X(TH)=1• X(TT)=0X(TT)=0
14. Countable & Uncountable Sets (Informal)Defining countable and uncountable sets are beyond the scope of our discussions. We shall give examples of such sets. • A set is countable if it is either countably finite or countably infinite• A se is uncountable if it is not countable Counting : In simple words, counting means arranging elements so each one appears at a definite position (1st, 2nd, 3rd, …). Examples :• The set {1,2,3,4}{1,2,3,4} is an example of countably finite set. We can list all the elements and the number of elements in this set is finite• The set of all the natural numbers is a countably infinite set. We can count the elements of the set, but there are infinitely many elements• The set of all the real numbers, denoted by RR, is an uncountable set. Between any two distinct real number, we can find another real number. So, it is not possible to list all the elements of RR• Any subset of a countable set is a countable set• Any interval of the form (a,b), [a,b),(a,b]
or
 [a,b],(a,b),[a,b),(a,b]or[a,b], where a<ba<b is an example of an uncountable set
15. Discrete Random VariableA discrete random variable takes values in a countable set. Probability Mass Function (PMF)• Let X:S→RX:S→R be a discrete random variable with TT as the range set. • For each u∈Tu∈T, let us define the event AuAu as followsAu={v∈S|X(v)=u}Au={v∈S|X(v)=u}• The probability mass function of XX is fX:T→[0,1]fX:T→[0,1], wherefX(u)=P(Au)fX(u)=P(Au) Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) - Discrete• Let X:S→RX:S→R be a discrete random variable with TT as the range set.• Let fX:T→[0,1]fX:T→[0,1] be the PMF of XX• For each u∈Tu∈T, let us define a set LuLu as followsLu={v∈T|v⩽u}Lu={v∈T|v⩽u}• The CDF of XX is FX:R→[0,1]FX:R→[0,1], whereFX(u)= ∑v∈LufX(v)=P(X⩽u)FX(u)=∑v∈LufX(v)=P(X⩽u)• If, for some u∈Ru∈R, Lu={}Lu={}, then FX(u)=0FX(u)=0• Suppose, TX={x1,...,xn}TX={x1,...,xn} and x1<x2<...<xnx1<x2<...<xn, Then,FX(xi)=P(X⩽xi)=i∑k=1fX(xk)FX(xi)=P(X⩽xi)=∑k=1ifX(xk) SupportLet XX be a discrete random variable with fXfX as its PMF. The support of XX is given by the following set.{x|fX(x)>0}{x|fX(x)>0} Common Distributions
DistributionParametersSupportPMF
X ~ BernoulliX~Bernoullip∈[0,1]p∈[0,1]x∈{0,1}x∈{0,1}afX(1)=pfX(0)=(1-p)fX(1)=pfX(0)=(1-p)
X ~ BinomialX~Binomialan∈{0,1,2...}p∈[0,1]n∈{0,1,2...}p∈[0,1]x∈{0,1,...,n}x∈{0,1,...,n}fX(x)=nCx(p)x(1-p)n-xfX(x)=nCx(p)x(1-p)n-x
X - PoissonX-Poisson𝜆>0𝜆>0x∈[0,∞)x∈[0,∞)fX(x)=e-𝜆𝜆x
x!fX(x)=e-𝜆𝜆xx!
X ~ GeometricX~Geometricp∈[0,1]p∈[0,1]x∈{1,2,...}x∈{1,2,...}fX(x)=(p)(1-p)x-1fX(x)=(p)(1-p)x-1
 Note - A Bernoulli random variable is also called an indicator random variable.
16. Continuous Random VariableA continuous random variable takes values over an interval and is described by a density function. Probability Density Function (PDF)• Let X:S→RX:S→R be a continuous random variable• The probability density function of XX is fX:R→[0,∞)fX:R→[0,∞)• For any a,b∈Ra,b∈R, where a⩽ba⩽b,P(a⩽X⩽b)=b∫afX(x) dxP(a⩽X⩽b)=∫abfX(x)dx• Note:P(X=a)=a∫afX(x) dx=0P(X=a)=∫aafX(x)dx=0 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) - Continuous• Let X:S→RX:S→R be a continuous random variable• The CDF of XX is FX:R→[0,1]FX:R→[0,1], whereFX(u)=u∫-∞fX(x) dxFX(u)=∫-∞ufX(x)dx• P(a⩽X⩽b)P(a⩽X⩽b) is given as followsP(a⩽X⩽b)=FX(b)-FX(a)=b∫afX(x) dxP(a⩽X⩽b)=FX(b)-FX(a)=∫abfX(x)dx• Relationship between CDF and PDFfX(x)=d
dxFX(x)fX(x)=ddxFX(x)• FXFX is a non-decreasing function, taking non-negative values• As x→-∞x→-∞, FX(x)→0FX(x)→0• As x→1x→1, FX(x)→1FX(x)→1 SupportLet XX be a continuous random variable with fXfX as its PDF. The support of XX is given by the following set.{x|fX(x)>0}{x|fX(x)>0} Common Distributions
DistributionParametersSupportPDF (for support)
X ~ Uniform(a,b)X~Uniform(a,b)a,b∈Ra,b∈R, a⩽ba⩽bx∈[a,b]x∈[a,b]fX(x)=1
b-afX(x)=1b-a
X ~ Exponential(𝜆)X~Exponential(𝜆)𝜆>0𝜆>0x∈[0,∞)x∈[0,∞)fX(x)=𝜆e-𝜆xfX(x)=𝜆e-𝜆x
X - Normal(𝜇,𝜎2)X-Normal(𝜇,𝜎2)𝜇∈R, 𝜎2⩾0𝜇∈R,𝜎2⩾0x∈Rx∈RfX(x)=1
2𝜋𝜎e-(x-𝜇)2
2𝜎2fX(x)=12𝜋𝜎e-(x-𝜇)22𝜎2
17. Joint DistributionDiscrete CaseXX and YY are two discrete random variables with TXTX and TYTY as their corresponding range set. The joint PMF of XX and YY, denoted by fXY:TX×TY→[0,1]fXY:TX×TY→[0,1] is given as followsfXY(x,y)=P(X=x,Y=y)fXY(x,y)=P(X=x,Y=y)Continuous CaseXX and YY are two continuous random variables. The joint PDF of XX and YY is given by fXY:R2→[0,∞)fXY:R2→[0,∞) ifP((X,Y)∈A)=∫∫AfXY(x,y) dx dyP((X,Y)∈A)=∫∫AfXY(x,y)dxdyfor every region AA in R2R2
18. Marginal DistributionDiscrete CaseXX and YY are two discrete random variables with fXYfXY as their joint PMF. The range of XX is TXTX and the range of YY is TYTY. The individual PMF of XX and YY are called the marginal PMFs. They are given as follows.fX(x)=P(X=x)= ∑y'∈TYfXY(x,y') fY(y)=P(Y=y)= ∑x'∈TYfXY(x',y)fX(x)=P(X=x)=∑y'∈TYfXY(x,y')fY(y)=P(Y=y)=∑x'∈TYfXY(x',y) Continuous CaseXX and YY are two continuous random variables with fXYfXY as their joint PDF. The individual PDF of XX and YY are called the marginal PDFs. They are given as follows.fX(x)=∞∫-∞fXY(x,y') dy fY(y)=∞∫-∞fXY(x',y) dxfX(x)=∫-∞∞fXY(x,y')dyfY(y)=∫-∞∞fXY(x',y)dx
19. Conditional DistributionDiscrete CaseXX and YY are two discrete random variables with fXfX and fYfY as their PMFs. The joint PMF of XX and YY is fXYfXY. The conditional PMF of XX given that Y=y'Y=y' is fX|Y=y'fX|Y=y', wherefX|Y=y'(x)=fXY(x,y')
fY(y')=P(X=x|Y=y')fX|Y=y'(x)=fXY(x,y')fY(y')=P(X=x|Y=y')Continuous CaseXX and YY are two continuous random variables with fXfX and fYfY as their PDFs. The joint PDF of XX and YY is fXYfXY. The conditional PDF of XX given that Y=y'Y=y' is fX|Y=y'fX|Y=y', wherefX|Y=y'(x)=fXY(x,y')
fY(y')fX|Y=y'(x)=fXY(x,y')fY(y')
20. Independent Random VariablesDiscrete CaseXX and YY are two discrete random variables with fXfX and fYfY as their PMFs and TXTX and TYTY as their ranges. The joint PMF of XX and YY is fXYfXY. XX and YY are said to be independent, iffXY(x,y)=fX(x)·fY(y) ∀x∈TX & ∀y∈TYfXY(x,y)=fX(x)·fY(y)∀x∈TX&∀y∈TY Continuous CaseXX and YY are two continuous random variables with fXfX and fYfY as their PDFs. The joint PDF of XX and YY is fXYfXY. XX and YY are said to be independent, iffXY(x,y)=fX(x)·fY(y) ∀(x,y)∈R2fXY(x,y)=fX(x)·fY(y)∀(x,y)∈R2
21. Independent & Identically Distributed (iid) Random VariablesTwo random variables XX and YY are said to be independent and identically distributed if• they are independent• their PMFs are identical (for discrete)• their PDFs are identical (for continuous) Example : Suppose we toss two unbiased coins. Let XX and YY be the random variables denoting the results of coin 1 and coin 2 respectively. Let • X(
Heads
)=1=Y(
Heads
)X(Heads)=1=Y(Heads)• X(
Tails
)=1=Y(
Tails
)X(Tails)=1=Y(Tails) Independence• The result of a coin does not affect the result of the other. Therefore,– fX|Y=1(x)=fX(x)fX|Y=1(x)=fX(x)– fX|Y=0(x)=fX(x)fX|Y=0(x)=fX(x)– fY|X=1(y)=fY(y)fY|X=1(y)=fY(y)– fY|X=0(y)=fY(y)fY|X=0(y)=fY(y) Identically Distributed• The probability of heads is the same for both the coins– fX(1)=fY(1)=0.5fX(1)=fY(1)=0.5• The probability of tails is the same for both the coins– fX(0)=fY(0)=0.5fX(0)=fY(0)=0.5 Therefore, XX and YY are independent and identically distributed.
22. Expectation & VarianceDiscrete CaseXX is a discrete random variable with range TXTX and PMF fXfX.• Expectation of XXE[X]= ∑x∈TXfX(x)·xE[X]=∑x∈TXfX(x)·x• Variance of XXVar(X)=E[(X-E[X])2]=E[X2]-(E[X])2= ∑x∈TX[fX(x)·(x-E[X])2]Var(X)=E[(X-E[X])2]=E[X2]-(E[X])2=∑x∈TX[fX(x)·(x-E[X])2] Continuous CaseXX is a continuous random variable with PDF fXfX.• Expectation of XXE[X]=∞∫-∞fX(x)·x dxE[X]=∫-∞∞fX(x)·xdx• Variance of XXVar(X)=E[(X-E[X])2]=E[X2]-(E[X])2=∞∫-∞fX(x)·(x-E[X])2 dxVar(X)=E[(X-E[X])2]=E[X2]-(E[X])2=∫-∞∞fX(x)·(x-E[X])2dxStandard DeviationThe standard deviation of XX is given as follows::SD(X)=Var(X)SD(X)=Var(X) Linearity Of ExpectationXX and YY are two random variables. Consider Z=𝛼X+𝛽YZ=𝛼X+𝛽Y, where 𝛼,𝛽∈R𝛼,𝛽∈Ra
E[Z]=E[𝛼X+𝛽Y]
=𝛼E[X]+𝛽E[Y]
E[Z]=E[𝛼X+𝛽Y]=𝛼E[X]+𝛽E[Y] Independence & ExpectationSuppose, XX and YY are two independent random variables. ThenE[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y] Proof : X & YX&Y are discrete. Range of XX is TXTX and range of YY is TYTY
E[XY]= ∑x∈TX ∑y∈TYfXY(x,y)·xy
= ∑x∈TX ∑y∈TYfX(x)·fY(y)·xy
= ∑x∈TX[fX(x)·x( ∑y∈TYfY(y)·y)]
= ∑x∈TXE[Y]·(fX(x)·x)
=E[Y] ∑x∈TXfX(x)·x
=E[X]·E[Y]
E[XY]=∑x∈TX∑y∈TYfXY(x,y)·xy=∑x∈TX∑y∈TYfX(x)·fY(y)·xy=∑x∈TX[fX(x)·x(∑y∈TYfY(y)·y)]=∑x∈TXE[Y]·(fX(x)·x)=E[Y]∑x∈TXfX(x)·x=E[X]·E[Y] Proof : X & YX&Y are continuous.
E[XY]=∞∫-∞∞∫-∞fXY(x,y)·xy dy dx
=∞∫-∞∞∫-∞fX(x)·fY(y)·xy dy dx
=∞∫-∞fX(x)·x[∞∫-∞fY(y)·y dy ]dx
=∞∫-∞E[Y]·(fX(x)·x) dx
=E[Y]∞∫-∞fX(x)·x dx
=E[X]·E[Y]
E[XY]=∫-∞∞∫-∞∞fXY(x,y)·xydydx=∫-∞∞∫-∞∞fX(x)·fY(y)·xydydx=∫-∞∞fX(x)·x[∫-∞∞fY(y)·ydy]dx=∫-∞∞E[Y]·(fX(x)·x)dx=E[Y]∫-∞∞fX(x)·xdx=E[X]·E[Y] If XX and YY are independent, then E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]. However, having E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y] does not guarantee independence between XX and YY. Independence and VarianceIf two random variables XX and YY are independent, thenVar(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y) Proof :
Var(X+Y)=E[(X+Y)2]-(E[X+Y])2
=E[X2+Y2+2XY]-(E[X]+E[Y])2
=E[X2]+E[Y2]+2·E[XY]-(E[X])2-(E[Y])2-2·E[X]·E[Y]
=[E[X2]-(E[X])2]+[E[Y2]-(E[Y])2]+2(E[XY]-E[X]·E[Y])
=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2(E[X]·E[Y]-E[X]·E[Y])
=Var(X)+Var(Y)
Var(X+Y)=E[(X+Y)2]-(E[X+Y])2=E[X2+Y2+2XY]-(E[X]+E[Y])2=E[X2]+E[Y2]+2·E[XY]-(E[X])2-(E[Y])2-2·E[X]·E[Y]=[E[X2]-(E[X])2]+[E[Y2]-(E[Y])2]+2(E[XY]-E[X]·E[Y])=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2(E[X]·E[Y]-E[X]·E[Y])=Var(X)+Var(Y) A note on simplification,• E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y], since XX and YY are independent. Note : If XX and YY are independent, then Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y), but the converse need not be true. Conditional Expectation (Discrete)Suppose, XX and YY are two discrete random variables with fXfX and fYfY as their corresponding PMF. Let TXTX be the range of XX. The expectiation of XX, given Y=y'Y=y' isE[X|Y=y']= ∑x∈TXx·fX|Y=y'(x)E[X|Y=y']=∑x∈TXx·fX|Y=y'(x)Conditional Expectation (Continuous)Suppose, XX and YY are two continuous random variables with fXfX and fYfY as their corresponding PDF. The expectiation of XX, given Y=y'Y=y' isE[X|Y=y']=∞∫-∞x·fX|Y=y'(x) dxE[X|Y=y']=∫-∞∞x·fX|Y=y'(x)dx Expectation & Variance of Common Distributions
DISTRIBUTIONEXPECTATIONVARIANCE
Bernoulli(p)Bernoulli(p)ppp(1-p)p(1-p)
Binomial(n,p)Binomial(n,p)npnpnp(1-p)np(1-p)
Poisson(𝜆)Poisson(𝜆)𝜆𝜆𝜆𝜆
Geometric(p)Geometric(p)1/p1/p(1-p)/p2(1-p)/p2
Uniform(a,b)Uniform(a,b)(a+b)/2(a+b)/2(b-a)2/12(b-a)2/12
Exponential(𝜆)Exponential(𝜆)1/𝜆1/𝜆1/𝜆21/𝜆2
Normal(𝜇,𝜎2)Normal(𝜇,𝜎2)𝜇𝜇𝜎2𝜎2
23. Covariance & CorrelartionCovariance XX and YY are two random variables. Their covariance is given as follows::
Cov(X,Y)=EaaX-E[X]aaY-E[Y]aa
=EaXY-X·E[Y]-Y·E[X]+E[X]·E[Y]a
=E[XY]-E[Y]·E[X]-E[X]·E[Y]+E[X]·E[Y]]
=E[XY]-E[X]·E[Y]
Cov(X,Y)=E[(X-E[X])(Y-E[Y])]=E[XY-X·E[Y]-Y·E[X]+E[X]·E[Y]]=E[XY]-E[Y]·E[X]-E[X]·E[Y]+E[X]·E[Y]]=E[XY]-E[X]·E[Y]• Cov(X,Y)=Cov(Y,X)Cov(X,Y)=Cov(Y,X)• Cov(X,X)=Var(X)Cov(X,X)=Var(X)• Cov(X,Y)=0Cov(X,Y)=0 implies that the random variables XX and YY are uncorrelated• Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2·Cov(X,Y)Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)+2·Cov(X,Y) Linearity Of CovarianceX,Y
and
 ZX,YandZ are three random variables. Let 𝛼,𝛽∈R𝛼,𝛽∈R. Then• Cov(X,𝛼Y+𝛽Z) = 𝛼Cov(X,Y ) + 𝛽Cov(X,Z)Cov(X,𝛼Y+𝛽Z)=𝛼Cov(X,Y)+𝛽Cov(X,Z)• Cov(𝛼X+𝛽Y ,Z) = 𝛼Cov(X,Z) + 𝛽Cov(Y,Z)Cov(𝛼X+𝛽Y,Z)=𝛼Cov(X,Z)+𝛽Cov(Y,Z) CorrelationXX and YY are two random variables. Their correlation is given as follows::Corr(X,Y)=Cov(X,Y)
Var(X)·Var(Y)Corr(X,Y)=Cov(X,Y)Var(X)·Var(Y) Independence & CovarianceSuppose, XX and YY are two independent random variables. Then
Cov(X,Y)=0
Cov(X,Y)=0Proof :
Cov(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]·E[Y]
=E[X]·E[Y]-E[X]·E[Y]
=0
Cov(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]·E[Y]=E[X]·E[Y]-E[X]·E[Y]=0While independence implies that Cov(X,Y)=0Cov(X,Y)=0, Cov(X,Y)=0Cov(X,Y)=0 does not imply that XX and YY are independent.
